content:
**Executive summary:** Puberty: the developmental phase when a child's body undergoes physical changes leading to sexual maturity (ability to reproduce). Controlled by hormones from endocrine .

**Must know**
• Reproductive organs present from birth but become **functional only after puberty**.
• Hormones (testosterone in males, oestrogen/progesterone in females) drive changes.
• Puberty is natural; timing varies — not a disease.
**Puberty**
• the developmental phase when a child's body undergoes **physical changes** leading to **sexual maturity** (ability to reproduce). Controlled by **hormones** from endocrine glands.

**Typical age range:** girls **11–13 years**; boys **14–16 years** (individual variation is normal).

**Changes in boys:**
• Growth of **facial/body hair**, **deepening of voice** (larynx enlarges).
• **Broadening of shoulders**, increased muscle mass.
• Growth of **testes** and **penis**; production of **sperm** begins.
• **Adam's apple** becomes prominent.

**Changes in girls:**
• **Breast development**, widening of hips.
• Growth of **pubic/axillary hair**.
• **Menarche** — first menstruation; **ovaries** release egg monthly.
• Emotional and growth spurts.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Puberty & Sexual Maturity"?
• State: Reproductive organs present from birth but become functional only after pub?
• State: Hormones (testosterone in males, oestrogen/progesterone in females) drive c?
• Explain: Puberty is natural; timing varies?
• True or false trap: Puberty ≠ adolescence entirely, but overlaps; puberty is biological, adolescence is b?
• True or false trap: 'Voice breaks in girls' is FALSE — voice deepens markedly in boys.?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Sexual maturity — stage when gametes are produced and reproduction is possible.
• Menarche — first occurrence of menstruation in girls.
• Puberty ≠ adolescence entirely, but overlaps; puberty is biological, adolescence is broader psychosocial phase.
• 'Voice breaks in girls' is FALSE — voice deepens markedly in boys.
• Sperm production continues lifelong (with decline); eggs are limited (one per month after puberty).

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch3-02 — next topic
teacherTip: Boys 14–16, Girls 11–13 — BG before teen years mnemonic for typical ICSE age ranges.
examTip: List changes separately for boys and girls — examiners award marks only for gender-correct points; no mixing.

content:
**Executive summary:** Penis: copulatory organ; delivers semen into female reproductive tract.

**Must know**
• Testes = sperm + testosterone; located in scrotum for cool temperature.
• Millions of sperm produced daily after puberty.
• Semen nourishes and transports sperm — not the same as 'sperm' alone.
**Primary organs — testes (2):**
• Ovoid glands in **scrotum** (outside body cavity) — lower temperature needed for sperm formation.
• Produce **sperm** (male gametes) and hormone **testosterone**.

**Ducts and glands:**
• **Epididymis** — coiled tube on testis; sperm mature and are stored.
• **Vas deferens (sperm duct)** — carries sperm from epididymis upward.
• **Seminal vesicles** + **prostate gland** — add nutritive fluid (fructose, enzymes, alkaline secretion) forming **semen**; neutralises vaginal acidity.
• **Urethra** — common passage for urine and semen (not simultaneously); passes through **penis**.

**Penis**
• copulatory organ; delivers semen into female reproductive tract.

**Sperm structure (overview):** head (nucleus), middle piece (mitochondria for energy), tail (flagellum for motility).
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Male Reproductive System"?
• Explain: Testes?
• State: Millions of sperm produced daily after puberty.?
• Explain: Semen nourishes and transports sperm?
• True or false trap: 'Urethra carries only semen' is FALSE — also urine.?
• Exam focus: Label diagram: testis, epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicle, prostate, urethra, penis?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Gamete — haploid reproductive cell (sperm in male).
• Scrotum — pouch holding testes outside abdominal cavity.
• Sperm produced in testes; matured/stored in epididymis.
• 'Urethra carries only semen' is FALSE — also urine.
• Testes are endocrine AND exocrine (hormone + sperm).

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• 1 → Seminal vesicle — produces a secretion that serves as a medium for transporting sperms.
• Sperms are produced in the testes.
• (a) Ovary — produces eggs (ova) and secretes the reproductive hormones oestrogen and progesterone.
• (a) Ovary (b) Vagina (c) Uterus (d) Fallopian tubes (e) Cervix
• (a) A sperm and an ovum are unicellular.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch3-01 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch3-03 — next topic
teacherTip: Testes in scrotum = Temperature control for sperm — 'cool bag' outside body.
examTip: Label diagram: testis, epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicle, prostate, urethra, penis — sequence of sperm pathway is frequently asked.

content:
**Executive summary:** Cervix: narrow lower neck of uterus opening into vagina.

**Must know**
• Egg produced in ovary → oviduct (fertilisation) → uterus (implantation/growth).
• Internal fertilisation — fusion occurs inside female body in oviduct.
• Uterus nourishes developing baby; vagina is not where fertilisation occurs.
**Primary organs — ovaries (2):**
• Almond-sized; produce **egg (ovum)** — one released roughly **every 28 days** (ovulation).
• Secrete **oestrogen** and **progesterone**.

**Oviducts (Fallopian tubes):**
• Funnel-shaped end near ovary (fimbriae); **site of fertilisation**.
• Egg travels through tube toward uterus; ciliated lining aids movement.

**Uterus (womb):**
• Pear-shaped, muscular, thick-walled.
• Lining (**endometrium**) thickens monthly to receive embryo; sheds during menstruation if no pregnancy.

**Cervix**
• narrow lower neck of uterus opening into **vagina**.

**Vagina**
• muscular tube; receives penis during mating; birth canal; passage for menstrual flow.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Female Reproductive System"?
• State: Egg produced in ovary → oviduct (fertilisation) → uterus (implantation/grow?
• Explain: Internal fertilisation?
• State: Uterus nourishes developing baby; vagina is not where fertilisation occurs.?
• True or false trap: 'Fertilisation in uterus' is FALSE — oviduct.?
• Exam focus: Match structure to function: ovary–egg/hormones, oviduct–fertilisation, uterus–implantatio?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Ovulation — release of mature egg from ovary.
• Endometrium — inner lining of uterus.
• Oviduct = Fallopian tube (same structure, two names).
• 'Fertilisation in uterus' is FALSE — oviduct.
• One egg per cycle typically; sperm number in millions per ejaculation.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• (a) Ovary (b) Vagina (c) Uterus (d) Fallopian tubes (e) Cervix

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch3-02 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch3-04 — next topic
teacherTip: O-F-U-V path: Ovary produces egg → Fallopian tube (fertilisation) → Uterus (baby grows) → Vagina (birth canal).
examTip: Match structure to function: ovary–egg/hormones, oviduct–fertilisation, uterus–implantation, vagina–copulation/birth.

content:
**Executive summary:** 1.  During mating, semen deposited in vagina; millions of sperm swim through cervix → uterus → oviduct.

**Must know**
• Internal fertilisation = inside female body (vs external in frogs/fish).
• Only ONE sperm fuses with ONE egg.
• Zygote is diploid (46 chromosomes in humans); gametes are haploid (23 each).
**Fertilisation in humans**
• **internal**; occurs in the **oviduct (Fallopian tube)**, not in uterus or ovary.

**Sequence:**

**Zygote**
• first cell of new individual; begins **mitotic divisions** while moving toward uterus → becomes ball of cells (**morula** → **blastocyst**).
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Arteries vs Veins vs Capillaries**
| Vessel | Wall | Valves | Blood direction |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Artery | Thick, elastic | No (except near heart) | Away from heart |
| Vein | Thinner | Yes — prevent backflow | Towards heart |
| Capillary | One cell thick | No | Exchange in tissues |


**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Fertilisation — From Gametes to Zygote"?
• Explain: Internal fertilisation?
• State: Only ONE sperm fuses with ONE egg.?
• State: Zygote is diploid (46 chromosomes in humans); gametes are haploid (23 each)?
• True or false trap: 'Many sperm fertilise one egg' is FALSE — only one succeeds.?
• Exam focus: State 'internal fertilisation in oviduct' as a complete phrase — half marks lost if only '?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Zygote — diploid cell formed by fusion of sperm and egg.
• Internal fertilisation — gamete fusion inside the mother's body.
• Fertilisation site = oviduct; implantation site = uterus wall — don't confuse.
• 'Many sperm fertilise one egg' is FALSE — only one succeeds.
• Egg is much larger than sperm; sperm contributes mainly nucleus + tail for movement.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch3-03 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch3-05 — next topic
teacherTip: Fertilisation in the Fallopian tube — 'F' and 'F' match; Implantation in Uterus — 'I' and 'U' don't match, so they're different places.
examTip: State 'internal fertilisation in oviduct' as a complete phrase — half marks lost if only 'internal' is written without location.

content:
**Executive summary:** After fertilisation, the dividing embryo reaches the uterus (~6–7 days) and embeds in the thickened endometrium — process called implantation.

**Must know**
• Implantation = embedding in uterine wall, NOT in oviduct.
• Pregnancy begins at successful implantation.
• Endometrium is richly supplied with blood — nourishes embryo initially.
**Stages (simplified):**
• Zygote → repeated mitosis → **morula** (solid ball) → **blastocyst** (hollow ball with inner cell mass).
• Blastocyst burrows into endometrial lining → **implantation**.
• Inner cell mass develops into **embryo**; outer cells help form **placenta**.

**If implantation fails or does not occur:** thickened endometrium breaks down → **menstruation**.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Implantation & Early Embryo Development"?
• Explain: Implantation?
• State: Pregnancy begins at successful implantation.?
• Explain: Endometrium is richly supplied with blood?
• True or false trap: 'Embryo develops in oviduct' is FALSE — only early cleavage during transit.?
• Exam focus: Distinguish embryo (early development) from foetus (later stage with recognisable organs) ?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Implantation — attachment and embedding of blastocyst into uterine lining.
• Embryo — developing organism from zygote until major organs form (in humans, roughly first 8 weeks).
• Fertilisation (oviduct) happens days before implantation (uterus).
• 'Embryo develops in oviduct' is FALSE — only early cleavage during transit.
• Ectopic pregnancy — implantation outside uterus (often oviduct) — dangerous, not normal.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• Incorrect statement: Understand fertilisation, implantation, embryo development and gestation;.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch3-04 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch3-06 — next topic
teacherTip: Fertilise in the Tube, Implant in the Uterus — two locations, two different T-words to remember.
examTip: Distinguish embryo (early development) from foetus (later stage with recognisable organs) — ICSE may use 'embryo' for initial implantation phase.

content:
**Executive summary:** Placenta: disc-shaped temporary organ formed from embryonic and uterine tissues; embedded in uterine wall during pregnancy.

**Must know**
• Foetal and maternal blood do NOT mix directly — exchange across thin placental membranes (diffusion).
• Umbilical cord is cut at birth; scar becomes navel (belly button).
• Placenta is expelled after birth (afterbirth).
**Placenta**
• disc-shaped temporary organ formed from **embryonic** and **uterine** tissues; embedded in uterine wall during pregnancy.

**Functions:**
• **Exchange** of **oxygen** and **nutrients** from mother to embryo/foetus.
• Removal of **carbon dioxide** and **waste (urea)** from foetus to mother's blood.
• **Barrier** — filters some harmful substances; allows antibodies (IgG) to pass giving passive immunity.
• Produces **hormones** (progesterone, oestrogen, hCG) to maintain pregnancy.

**Umbilical cord**
• connects foetus to placenta; contains **two umbilical arteries** (carry deoxygenated blood/wastes to placenta) and **one umbilical vein** (carries oxygenated, nutrient-rich blood to foetus).
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Placenta & Umbilical Cord"?
• Explain: Foetal and maternal blood do NOT mix directly?
• State: Umbilical cord is cut at birth; scar becomes navel (belly button).?
• State: Placenta is expelled after birth (afterbirth).?
• True or false trap: 2 arteries + 1 vein in cord — 'two veins' is FALSE.?
• Exam focus: List placenta functions as exchange + hormone secretion + barrier — three-function answer ?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Placenta — temporary organ linking mother and foetus for nourishment and gas exchange.
• Umbilical cord — lifeline connecting foetus to placenta.
• 2 arteries + 1 vein in cord — 'two veins' is FALSE.
• Arteries carry waste AWAY from foetus; vein brings good blood TO foetus.
• Placenta prevents some but not all pathogens/drugs — thalidomide tragedy example.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• The PLACENTA is a temporary organ that connects the developing foetus to the mother's uterine wall.
• (a) Ovary — produces eggs (ova) and secretes the reproductive hormones oestrogen and progesterone.
• Incorrect statement: After fertilisation, the zygote divides to form an embryo and later a foetus during the gestation period of about nine months.
• 1 → Seminal vesicle — produces a secretion that serves as a medium for transporting sperms.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch3-05 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch3-07 — next topic
teacherTip: Vein to Victory (good blood to baby); Arteries Away with waste — one Vein, two Arteries.
examTip: List placenta functions as exchange + hormone secretion + barrier — three-function answer for 3 marks.

content:
**Executive summary:** Gestation (pregnancy): period of development from implantation to birth; in humans approximately 9 months (38–40 weeks / ~280 days).

**Must know**
• Gestation ≈ 9 months; measured from last menstrual period often as 40 weeks clinically.
• Menstruation = breakdown of unused endometrium, not 'bad blood'.
• Cycle continues from puberty (menarche) until menopause.
**Gestation (pregnancy)**
• period of development from **implantation** to **birth**; in humans approximately **9 months (38–40 weeks / ~280 days)**.

**Development overview:**
• Embryo → all major organs form (first trimester critical).
• Foetus grows rapidly; movements felt mid-pregnancy.
• Fully developed foetus born through rhythmic **uterine contractions** dilating **cervix**.

**Menstrual cycle (non-pregnant, reproductive-age female):**
• Average **28 days**; governed by hormones (oestrogen, progesterone, FSH, LH).
• **Day 1** — menstruation begins (shedding of endometrium through vagina; 3–5 days).
• Mid-cycle (~day 14) — **ovulation** (egg released from ovary).
• After ovulation, endometrium thickens again preparing for possible implantation.
• If no fertilisation → hormone drop → endometrium sheds → next period.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Gestation & Menstrual Cycle"?
• State: Gestation ≈ 9 months; measured from last menstrual period often as 40 weeks?
• Explain: Menstruation?
• State: Cycle continues from puberty (menarche) until menopause.?
• True or false trap: Ovulation ≠ menstruation — ovulation is egg release (~day 14); menstruation is sheddi?
• True or false trap: 'Menstrual cycle stops permanently after one pregnancy' is FALSE — resumes unless bre?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Gestation — carrying and development of embryo/foetus in uterus.
• Menstruation — periodic discharge of blood and endometrial tissue when pregnancy does not occur.
• Ovulation ≠ menstruation — ovulation is egg release (~day 14); menstruation is shedding (~day 1).
• 'Menstrual cycle stops permanently after one pregnancy' is FALSE — resumes unless breastfeeding/other factors delay.
• Menopause — permanent end of menstrual cycles (~45–55 years).

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• (a) Ovary — produces eggs (ova) and secretes the reproductive hormones oestrogen and progesterone.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch3-06 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch3-08 — next topic
teacherTip: 28-day cycle: Day 1 = Period starts, Day 14 = Ovulation (egg released) — two anchor points for MCQs.
examTip: Explain why menstruation occurs — 'no fertilisation, progesterone falls, endometrium breaks down' — cause-effect marking scheme.

content:
**Executive summary:** Personal hygiene during adolescence: essential because hormonal changes increase sweat, oil secretion, and reproductive tract sensitivity.

**Must know**
• Menstruation is normal physiological process — not impure; stigma is unscientific.
• Poor menstrual hygiene risks infections (RTI).
• Adolescence needs emotional support as well as physical hygiene.
**Personal hygiene during adolescence**
• essential because hormonal changes increase sweat, oil secretion, and reproductive tract sensitivity.

**Daily practices:**
• **Bathe regularly**; wash armpits, groin, feet — reduces bacterial growth and body odour.
• **Clean genital area** with water; girls wipe front to back after toilet to prevent urinary/reproductive infections.
• **Change underwear** daily; use clean absorbent pads/cloth during menstruation; dispose or wash sanitary material properly.
• **Oral hygiene** — brush twice daily; hormonal changes can affect gums.

**Menstrual hygiene:**
• Use sanitary pads/tampons/menstrual cups; change pads every 4–6 hours.
• Hands washed before and after changing protection.
• Balanced diet and iron-rich foods during periods to prevent anaemia.

**General reproductive health:**
• Nutritious diet, exercise, adequate sleep support healthy development.
• Avoid tobacco, alcohol, drugs — damage developing organs and gametes.
• Consult trusted adult/doctor for unusual pain, discharge, or cycle irregularities.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Personal Hygiene & Reproductive Health"?
• Explain: Menstruation is normal physiological process?
• State: Poor menstrual hygiene risks infections (RTI).?
• State: Adolescence needs emotional support as well as physical hygiene.?
• True or false trap: Hygiene prevents infection; it does not 'stop' menstruation.?
• True or false trap: 'Bathing during periods is harmful' is FALSE — myth.?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Personal hygiene — practices maintaining health and cleanliness of body.
• Reproductive health — state of physical, emotional well-being related to reproductive system.
• Hygiene prevents infection; it does not 'stop' menstruation.
• 'Bathing during periods is harmful' is FALSE — myth.
• Clean water and sanitation are public health issues linked to adolescent girls' school attendance.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch3-07 — previous topic
teacherTip: Hygiene Habit loop: Clean body, Clean clothes, Clean menstrual products, Clean hands — four C's.
examTip: List practical hygiene measures for adolescents — ICSE value-based questions award marks for specific actionable points, not vague 'stay clean'.
